source.title: The hippocampal sharp wave-ripple in memory retrieval for immediate use and consolidation

confidence_rationale: Authoritative review of the canonical SWR substrate and its dual role (retrieval-for-immediate-use vs consolidation). Strong source quality (Nature Reviews Neuroscience, Frank lab). Mapping fidelity is moderate -- the review describes that SWRs route hippocampal content to downstream cortical targets, but does NOT describe a categorical write-gate that distinguishes 'rule applied during simulation/replay' from 'rule applied during waking action' in the specific REE sense (a per-rule tag carried by the simulated trajectory that suppresses the post-commit write channel). The biological substrate has the timing and the routing machinery REE needs, but the literature treats SWR-content downstream effects as continuous strength modulation, not as a discrete simulation-mode tag. Evidence direction MIXED because the substrate exists but the specific REE mechanism (categorical gate) is not what the literature reports.
failure_signatures: Joo & Frank treat SWR-mediated hippocampal-to-cortical communication as a continuous-strength routing event, not as a categorical simulation-vs-action distinction in the receiving cortex's write rules.
The review does not describe any biological substrate that suppresses arbitration-weight updates specifically during replay relative to waking action.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Hippocampal sharp wave-ripples (SWRs) are events of highly synchronous neural firing during which sequences of place-cell activity replay past or potential future spatial trajectories. SWRs serve a dual role: they route hippocampal content to downstream regions for immediate use (decision-making, planning, recollection) and they initiate memory consolidation. Disrupting SWRs impairs spatial working memory and consolidation; biased SWR content alters subsequent behaviour.
mapping.ree_translation: REE's MECH-094 hypothesis-tag and the candidate MECH-319 simulation-mode write-gate posit that rule-application during simulation/replay carries a categorical tag that suppresses arbitration-weight updates which would otherwise be triggered if the same rule were applied during waking action. Joo & Frank's review confirms that the SWR substrate exists, that it operates in both awake-quiescent and sleep states, and that its content can be intervened upon -- this is the biological substrate REE would need. But the review's framing of downstream effects is continuous (strength modulation, association formation) rather than the categorical-tag form REE proposes.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The review describes a dual-purpose substrate (immediate use + consolidation) but does not test or report whether downstream regions categorically distinguish replay-driven inputs from waking-driven inputs at the level of write-rule eligibility. Closest the literature comes is the observation that SWR-coordinated cortical responses are temporally compressed relative to waking experience -- this MIGHT serve as an implicit tag but the review does not frame it that way. Transfer risk is therefore moderate-to-high: REE may be proposing a function the substrate does not actually serve, OR REE may be naming a function that exists but has not yet been characterised at the cell/synaptic level.
mapping.source_context: Review paper covering rodent (primarily) and primate hippocampal SWR literature, single-unit recording + closed-loop disruption + content-decoding. No direct REE-relevant computational model.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality is high (canonical review). Mapping fidelity is moderate -- the substrate is right but the specific REE mechanism (categorical write-gate based on simulation-mode tag) is not described in the form REE needs. Transfer risk is real because REE's claim is more specific than what the literature has tested.
